facts: The Sun is about 109 times wider than the Earth
It would take 332,000 Earths to fill the Sun's volume, roughly the size of 244 billion football fields
The distance from New York to Los Angeles is about 2,470 miles, but the Sun is about 400 times farther from the Earth, at 93 million miles
idea.payoff: and we still don't know what makes up 99.8% of our solar system
idea.title: Sun vs Earth 🌞
idea.description: The Sun is our star, but how massive is it really? 🌞 Imagine a sphere so large that 109 Earths could fit side by side across it. Yet, despite its enormity, there's still so much we don't understand about our solar system 🤔. Watch to discover the jaw-dropping scale of the Sun and the mysteries that remain.

scenes.text: Sun vs. Earth: Prepare to be amazed!
scenes.visual: Starts with Earth, then zooms out rapidly to show a massive Sun appearing, making Earth tiny. An illustration of 109 Earths fitting across the Sun's diameter briefly flashes.
An animated graphic showing Earth, then the Sun appearing next to it, with a '109x' label highlighting the vast difference in diameter.

A large hollow Sun outline. Thousands of tiny Earths rapidly pour in to fill its volume. A comparison graphic showing the total volume being equivalent to 244 billion football fields fades in momentarily.

scenes.text: Sun is 93 Million Miles from Earth!
scenes.visual: Earth and Sun shown with a vast empty space between them. A line appears connecting them with '93,000,000 Miles' labeled. A smaller animated graphic depicting the distance from New York to Los Angeles (2,470 miles) appears briefly for scale.

scenes.text: And we still don't know what makes up 99.8% of our solar system
scenes.visual: The Sun glows intensely. Then, a quick zoom out to show the Sun and planets as small specks in a vast, dark, cosmic void, emphasizing the unknown and mystery. A faint question mark or '?' symbol appears.
